channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Osaka University and Fixstars Break 40-Qubit Barrier in Quantum Chemistry Simulation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a significant milestone in classical quantum simulation, with Osaka University and Fixstars successfully modeling quantum chemistry problems at the 40-qubit scale using advanced classical computing techniques. While this doesn't involve actual quantum hardware, classical simulation at this scale is crucial for validating quantum algorithms and understanding the computational complexity boundaries where quantum computers will eventually demonstrate clear advantages. The achievement provides valuable benchmarks for near-term quantum devices and helps researchers better understand how quantum chemistry simulations scale with system size, particularly for drug discovery and materials science applications. This work also highlights the ongoing "quantum simulation race" where classical computers continue pushing their limits, ultimately defining more precisely where quantum supremacy will emerge in practical applications.

Large-scale classical simulation of IQPE quantum circuits demonstrated in this work. Larger qubit counts and more Hamiltonian terms result in deeper circuits and longer simulation times. Credit: QIQB, The University of Osaka Osaka University’s Center for Quantum Information and Quantum Biology (QIQB) and Fixstars Corporation have established a new benchmark in classical quantum simulation, utilizing [...] The post Osaka University and Fixstars Break 40-Qubit Barrier in Quantum Chemistry Simulation appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Rigetti Sells Novera QPU to University of Saskatchewan for Canada’s First Open-Architecture System
item.description:
Curator's Take: This acquisition represents a significant milestone for quantum computing democratization, as the University of Saskatchewan becomes Canada's first institution to own a complete, open-architecture quantum system rather than merely accessing cloud-based services. Rigetti's 9-qubit Novera QPU installation demonstrates the growing viability of on-premises quantum systems for research institutions, potentially accelerating hands-on quantum education and experimental research capabilities across Canadian academia. The open-architecture design is particularly noteworthy, as it allows researchers to modify and experiment with the full quantum stack rather than being limited to predetermined software interfaces, fostering deeper understanding and innovation in quantum algorithm development.

Rigetti Computing (Nasdaq: RGTI) has officially expanded its on-premises footprint with the sale of a 9-qubit Novera™ QPU to the University of Saskatchewan (USask). This acquisition marks a historic milestone as USask becomes home to the first university-owned and operated, vendor-supported, full-stack, open-architecture quantum computer in Canada. The system is housed within USask’s Centre for [...] The post Rigetti Sells Novera QPU to University of Saskatchewan for Canada’s First Open-Architecture System appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Infleqtion and Safran Launch Integrated Quantum Timing Solution for GNSS-Challenged Environments
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Infleqtion and Safran represents a significant step toward commercializing quantum sensing technology for real-world applications where GPS signals are unreliable or unavailable. By integrating quantum optical clocks with proven synchronization platforms, the collaboration addresses a critical vulnerability in modern infrastructure that relies heavily on satellite-based timing systems. The focus on defense and telecommunications sectors highlights how quantum technologies are moving beyond research labs to solve immediate operational challenges, particularly in environments where adversaries might jam or spoof GPS signals. This type of quantum-classical hybrid approach could become a template for making other quantum sensing technologies commercially viable in the near term.

Infleqtion (NYSE: INFQ), a specialist in neutral-atom quantum technology, and Safran Electronics & Defense have announced the commercial availability of a quantum-enabled precision timing solution. The system integrates Infleqtion’s Tiqker™ quantum optical clock with Safran’s established White Rabbit and SecureSync® synchronization platforms. Aimed at defense, telecommunications, and critical infrastructure sectors, the solution is designed to [...] The post Infleqtion and Safran Launch Integrated Quantum Timing Solution for GNSS-Challenged Environments appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Is There a Silver Lining Behind The Looming Dark Clouds of Quantum’s Crypto-busting Powers?
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial shift in how we should view quantum computing's timeline and impact beyond the widely-discussed threat to current cryptography. While Google's recent findings have understandably focused attention on when quantum computers might break existing encryption methods, the same research suggests that practical quantum applications across multiple domains could emerge much earlier than previously anticipated. The development represents a classic double-edged sword in quantum computing: the very advances that pose security challenges also accelerate the arrival of beneficial quantum applications in optimization, simulation, and machine learning. This reframing is particularly important for organizations planning their quantum strategies, as it suggests the technology's positive impacts may materialize well before the full-scale cryptographic disruption that dominates current headlines.

Insider Brief The alarm over Google Quantum AI’s latest findings has centered on risk, but the same advance is sharpening the case that useful quantum applications may arrive sooner than expected. A recent white paper written by a Google-led research team outlined how a sufficiently large quantum system — on the order of hundreds of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Using Digital Twin Methods to Accurately Simulate Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights an important advancement in quantum error correction simulation through digital twin technology, which creates precise virtual replicas of quantum systems to model real-world imperfections like crosstalk between qubits. The ability to accurately simulate a distance-7 surface code with 97 qubits represents a significant step toward understanding how quantum error correction will perform on actual hardware, where noise and component variations can dramatically impact performance. Digital twins could prove essential for optimizing quantum error correction protocols before deployment on expensive quantum hardware, potentially accelerating the path to fault-tolerant quantum computers by identifying design flaws and performance bottlenecks in simulation rather than through costly trial-and-error on physical systems.

Diagram of a distance‑7 rotated surface code with 97 physical qubits (49 data qubits + 48 measurement qubits) modelled by the team. Grey nodes are data qubits; labeled circles are ancillas measuring X- or Z-type checks on neighboring data qubits. Edge colors encode the residual ZZ crosstalk strength (darker edges indicate stronger crosstalk).Credit: Quantum Elements [...] The post Using Digital Twin Methods to Accurately Simulate Quantum Error Correction appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Decryption Threshold — Re-estimating the Quantum Threat to Blockchain Infrastructure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing practical questions: exactly how many logical qubits and quantum operations would be needed to break the cryptographic foundations protecting Bitcoin and other blockchain networks. Recent estimates from Google Quantum AI and newcomer Oratomic are providing more refined calculations for cracking 256-bit elliptic curve cryptography, the security backbone of most cryptocurrency systems. While we're still likely years away from fault-tolerant quantum computers reaching these thresholds, these updated resource estimates are crucial for cryptocurrency developers and security experts planning the transition to quantum-resistant encryption schemes. The research represents an important intersection of quantum algorithm optimization and real-world cryptographic security, offering a clearer timeline for when blockchain infrastructure will need quantum-safe upgrades.

Comparison of logical quantum resources (number of logical qubits and Toffoli gates) required to break 256-bit ECDLP for the secp256k1 curve, as reported by various prior works. Credit: Google Quantum AI whitepaper. By Mohamed Abdel-Kareem Recent technical disclosures from Google Quantum AI and the newly emerged Oratomic have provided updated resource estimates for executing Shor’s [...] The post The Decryption Threshold — Re-estimating the Quantum Threat to Blockchain Infrastructure appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Palm Beach State College Takes Aim at Quantum Workforce Demand
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a critical shift in quantum computing's evolution from purely academic research to practical implementation requiring a diverse workforce beyond PhD-level scientists. As quantum technologies transition from laboratory demonstrations to real-world applications, the field desperately needs technicians, operators, and skilled workers who can build, maintain, and operate quantum systems in commercial settings. Palm Beach State College's initiative represents an important recognition that community colleges and vocational training programs will play a crucial role in developing the quantum workforce pipeline, potentially democratizing access to this emerging field. This workforce development approach could be the key to scaling quantum technologies from promising prototypes to widespread industrial deployment.

Insider Brief Quantum technology is often portrayed as a field staffed by people with doctoral degrees and years of academic experience. That may be how it is now, but experts are increasingly concerned that as quantum moves out of theory and into practice and out of the laboratory and into the real world, its success […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Routing Entanglement in Complex Quantum Networks Using GHZ States
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum networking's most practical challenges: how to efficiently route entangled photons through complex, real-world networks where connections inevitably fail. The authors' hybrid approach cleverly combines traditional Bell state measurements with more sophisticated GHZ measurements, showing that the optimal routing strategy depends heavily on network topology rather than following a one-size-fits-all solution. By testing their methods on actual network infrastructures like SURFnet in the Netherlands, this work bridges the gap between theoretical quantum networking protocols and the messy realities of deploying quantum internet infrastructure. The finding that different network structures require tailored entanglement routing strategies provides crucial guidance for engineers designing the quantum internet of the future.

Distributing entanglement to distant parties in a network is a central task in quantum information processing and quantum networking. The sensitivity of entangled states to loss necessitates the use of entanglement routing strategies. Recently, a routing strategy using Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) measurements instead of Bell state measurements (BSM) has been proposed. We further this direction of research by explicitly considering the varying measurement success probabilities of GHZ measurements. Moreover, we extend the analysis beyond square grid networks to complex network models such as Waxman networks and scale-free networks, as well as SURFnet, a real-world network topology in the Netherlands. Taking into account the varying success probabilities, naive application of GHZ routing achieves rates much lower than the conventional BSM routing. Instead, we propose a hybrid GHZ-BSM routing strategy. The hybrid GHZ-BSM routing strategy outperforms BSM routing in square grid networks. In other networks, however, more sophisticated adaptations using global information are required.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Novel permanent magnet array geometries for scalable trapped-ion quantum computing in a laser-free entanglement architecture
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical scalability bottleneck in trapped-ion quantum computing by redesigning the magnetic field architecture needed for laser-free qubit operations. The novel permanent magnet array creates localized field gradients that allow ions to move freely in and out of entanglement zones, solving a major transport problem that has plagued previous designs where strong magnetic fields created three-dimensional "traps" for the ions. By eliminating the need for large electrical currents to generate these fields, this approach could dramatically simplify the engineering of large-scale Quantum Charge-Coupled Device systems, potentially enabling trapped-ion quantum computers with thousands of qubits. The design's tolerance for mechanical misalignment also addresses practical manufacturing and maintenance challenges that become increasingly important as quantum systems scale up.

A novel design is presented for a permanent magnet array to address specific challenges with scalable trapped-ion quantum computing systems. Design and optimization of this magnet geometry is motivated by concepts for large-scale Quantum Charge-Coupled Device (QCCD) architectures. This proposal is relevant to magnetic field gradient schemes for laser-free entanglement using long-wavelength radiation, and individual addressing based on spatially dependent, magnetic field sensitive qubits. This configuration generates a localized, asymmetric magnetic field, yielding a region for ion transport into and out of a strong magnetic field gradient, while minimizing the absolute field experienced by the ion. This is a distinct improvement for scalability over dipolar magnet geometries where a strong magnetic field surrounds a magnetic field nil in three dimensions, which is problematic for ion transport applications. The design also relaxes the alignment constraints for experimental setup by allowing greater tolerance to misalignment in two dimensions. Additionally, the potential to scale a permanent magnet scheme in QCCD systems circumvents engineering challenges associated with using large electrical currents to generate the field gradient. Finally, a conceptual discussion is given for incorporating the design into a scalable QCCD type architecture.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SpinQ Technology Secures Nearly 1 Billion Chinese Yuan in Series C Funding to Scale Industrial Superconducting Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This substantial funding round positions SpinQ as one of the most well-capitalized quantum hardware companies globally, with nearly $139 million in Series C funding alone demonstrating serious investor confidence in their superconducting quantum approach. SpinQ's focus on full-stack solutions and industrial applications sets them apart in a market often dominated by pure research plays, suggesting growing demand for practical quantum systems that can be deployed in real-world settings. The Chinese investment represents a significant bet on competing with Western quantum leaders like IBM and Google, while SpinQ's emphasis on superconducting technology aligns with proven scalable architectures. This funding level indicates the quantum computing industry is entering a more mature phase where substantial capital is flowing toward companies with clear paths to commercialization rather than just technological demonstrations.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SpinQ Technology, a leading global provider of full-stack quantum computing solutions, today announced the successful completion of its 600 million Chinese Yuan (about $83 million U.S.) Series C+ financing round. This milestone brings the company’s total Series C funding to nearly 1 billion Chinese Yuan (about $139 million U.S.) within […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Generation of magnonic squeezed state and its superposition in a hybrid qubit-magnon system
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research demonstrates a novel pathway to create quantum squeezed states in magnonic systems using hybrid qubit-magnon architectures, achieving impressive 8 dB noise reduction that rivals performance in established quantum platforms like trapped ions and superconducting circuits. The work is particularly significant because it shows how to generate superpositions of these squeezed magnonic states, opening possibilities for quantum error correction schemes tailored specifically to magnonic platforms—an emerging frontier that could offer unique advantages for quantum sensing and memory applications. The achievement of clear phase-space interference fringes in these superposition states represents a crucial step toward using magnons as quantum information carriers, potentially bridging the gap between traditional electronics and quantum technologies in ways that could enable new hybrid quantum devices.

We propose a protocol for generating magnonic squeezed states (MSS) and their superpositions (SMSS) in a hybrid system comprising a superconducting flux qubit magnetically coupled to the Kittel mode of a yttrium iron garnet (YIG) sphere. The flux qubit provides an intrinsic longitudinal interaction with the magnon mode, which, under resonant microwave driving, gives rise to an effective qubit-state-dependent squeezing Hamiltonian. Numerical simulations incorporating realistic dissipation demonstrate that magnon quadrature noise reduction exceeding $8~\mathrm{dB}$ is achievable with experimentally accessible parameters.~By preparing the qubit in a superposition state followed by projective measurement, we further obtain symmetric and antisymmetric superpositions of orthogonally squeezed magnon states exhibiting clear phase-space interference fringes.~We discuss how the fourfold rotational symmetry of these states supports a bosonic logical encoding with potential for protecting against dominant error channels in magnonic platforms.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coinbase CEO Addresses ‘Quantum Threat’ to Cryptocurrencies
item.description:
Curator's Take: This represents a significant shift in how the cryptocurrency industry views quantum computing threats, moving from theoretical concern to active engineering priority. Armstrong's announcement comes as quantum error correction milestones from companies like Google and IBM suggest that cryptographically relevant quantum computers may arrive sooner than previously expected, potentially within the next 10-15 years rather than decades. The urgency is well-founded since Bitcoin and most cryptocurrencies rely on elliptic curve cryptography that could be broken by a sufficiently powerful quantum computer using Shor's algorithm. This corporate leadership on quantum-resistant cryptography could accelerate broader adoption of post-quantum security measures across the financial technology sector.

Insider Brief A steady stream of announcements that quantum teams are making progress on quantum error correction seems to have stirred the once-skeptical cryptocurrency community. Coinbase CEO and co-founder Brian Armstrong said he will personally lead an industry push to make Bitcoin quantum-resistant, marking a shift from long-term concern to near-term engineering priority after new […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Continuous-time evolution via probabilistic angle interpolation and its applications
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a clever approach to quantum simulation that sidesteps one of the field's most persistent challenges: Trotter errors that accumulate when breaking continuous time evolution into discrete steps. By using probabilistic angle interpolation in the continuous-time limit, the researchers have developed an algorithm that could make quantum simulations more accurate and resource-efficient, demonstrated through real experiments on Quantinuum's Reimei trapped-ion system. The practical applications shown here - from molecular energy calculations to studying exotic quantum correlations - highlight how this method could advance both quantum chemistry and fundamental physics research. What makes this particularly exciting is the combination of theoretical innovation with actual hardware validation, showing that sophisticated quantum algorithms are moving from concept to reality.

We explore the applicability of a stochastic time-evolution algorithm based on probabilistic angle interpolation. To simplify the pre-processing of the algorithm, we take the continuous-time limit, thereby explicitly eliminating Trotter errors and streamlining the resource analysis. We also introduce a noise-mitigation method tailored to it. As demonstrations, we apply the algorithm to two representative problems: estimating the ground-state energy of the $H_3^+$ molecular Hamiltonian and computing out-of-time-ordered correlators in the sparse Sachdev--Ye--Kitaev model. We evaluate the protocol's performance through numerical simulations and experiments on a trapped-ion quantum computer, Quantinuum Reimei.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Characterizing charge-parity detection based on an offset-charge-tunable transmon qubit via randomized benchmarking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article demonstrates a significant advancement in using superconducting qubits as ultra-sensitive charge detectors, achieving an impressive 99.37% fidelity in mapping charge-parity states onto qubit states through clever gate control techniques. The work represents a promising bridge between quantum computing hardware and fundamental physics research, as these charge-parity detection capabilities could enable searches for hypothetical dark matter particles and other rare events that interact weakly with matter. The ability to continuously monitor charge states with over 93% fidelity at microsecond timescales opens exciting possibilities for using quantum devices as precision sensors beyond their computational applications. While the researchers identified qubit readout as the current limiting factor, the achieved performance levels suggest that superconducting qubits could soon become practical tools for probing physics at the meV energy scale.

Superconducting qubits are compelling platforms for charge-parity detection and, due to their theoretical sensitivity on the meV energy scale, hold promise for rare event searches. In this work, we realize high-fidelity mapping of charge-parity states onto qubit states using an offset-charge-tunable transmon qubit and efficiently characterize the fidelity of the charge-parity detection via randomized benchmarking. Specifically, a gate control line is applied to control offset charge, allowing us to achieve the single-qubit gate fidelity up to 99.96%. We combine a net-zero-based pulse on the gate line with a spin-echo-based sequence to realize charge-parity mapping, achieving a fidelity of 99.37%. Then, we demonstrate continuous monitoring of the charge-parity state with over 93.4% fidelity at a 4-μs sampling interval. Finally, an error analysis of charge-parity detection is performed, and it is found that qubit readout is currently the largest source of error. We believe this work lays the foundation for future exploration of ultra-low energy particles.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Launches Open-Source Package to Simulate Logical Quantum Circuits at Scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: QuEra's open-source T-gate simulator represents a crucial breakthrough for quantum error correction research, addressing a major computational bottleneck that has slowed QEC development. The ability to efficiently simulate non-Clifford gates like the T-gate at scale is particularly significant because these operations are essential for universal quantum computing but notoriously expensive to simulate classically. By making this GPU-accelerated tool freely available to researchers, QuEra is democratizing access to the computational infrastructure needed to design and test fault-tolerant quantum algorithms before they can run on actual error-corrected hardware. This development could substantially accelerate the timeline for achieving practical quantum advantage by enabling more rapid iteration and validation of error correction schemes.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuEra Computing today open-sourced its T-gate simulator (Tsim), a GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulator that, for the first time, lets researchers simulate non-Clifford gate operations at the speed and scale that quantum error correction (QEC) development demands. QEC is the essential bridge between today’s noisy quantum processors and the fault-tolerant machines […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: RFOX (Rotated-Field Oscillatory eXchange) quantum algorithm: Towards Parameter-Free Quantum Optimizers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces RFOX, a promising new quantum optimization algorithm that maintains a consistently flat energy gap throughout its execution, addressing one of the most persistent challenges in quantum annealing where conventional methods often get trapped in vanishing energy gaps. The algorithm's parameter-free design and constant runtime scaling represent a significant theoretical advance, with experimental validation on IBM quantum processors showing up to an order of magnitude improvement in efficiency compared to standard approaches. What makes this particularly exciting is that RFOX performs better as problem difficulty increases, suggesting it could be especially valuable for the hard optimization problems where quantum computers are expected to show their greatest advantage. The combination of rigorous theoretical foundation through Floquet-Magnus analysis and practical hardware demonstration positions this as a potentially transformative approach for near-term quantum optimization applications.

We introduce RFOX (Rotated-Field Oscillatory eXchange), a parameter-free quantum algorithm for combinatorial optimization. RFOX combines an almost constant non-stoquastic $XX$ catalyst with a weak harmonic $ZX$ counter-diabatic term. Using the Floquet-Magnus expansion, we derive a closed-form effective Hamiltonian whose first-order term retains the full $XX$ driver, while the leading correction consists of a single qubit $Y$ field at high drive frequency. This structure ensures that the instantaneous spectral gap remains essentially flat, independent of both the interpolation parameter and the disorder strength, modulated only by a $δ$ parameter. This behavior stands in stark contrast to the unpredictable gap reductions, or even collapses, exhibited by the $X$ (stoquastic), $XX$, and $X+sXX$ (non-stoquastic) driver schedules. Extensive noiseless simulations on random-field Ising model (RFIM) instances with 7, 9, and 12 qubits, across three magnetic-field ranges, validate these spectral predictions: RFOX attains near-optimal, and in some cases exact, ground states using up to an order of magnitude fewer Trotter slices. Its performance advantage grows with increasing disorder, as conventional methods slow down near vanishing gaps, whereas RFOX maintains a constant runtime scaling of $T \propto Δ_{\min}^{-2}$. Hardware experiments on IBM Quantum processors (Eagle r3 and Heron r1, with 12, 15, and 20 physical qubits) reproduce similar performance rankings. These results suggest that fixed-gap, non-stoquastic drivers augmented with analytically derived counter-diabatic terms offer a promising, scalable, and tuning-free route toward quantum optimizers for combinatorial optimization problems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: AQ-Stacker: An Adaptive Quantum Matrix Multiplication Algorithm with Scaling via Parallel Hadamard Stacking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most fundamental computational bottlenecks in AI and machine learning by proposing a quantum algorithm that could dramatically accelerate matrix multiplication, the core operation behind everything from neural networks to scientific simulations. The key innovation lies in the "adaptive stacking" approach that allows the algorithm to dynamically scale its execution strategy based on available quantum hardware, making it potentially useful on both today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and tomorrow's fault-tolerant systems. While the theoretical complexity reduction to O(log N) for vector inner products is impressive, the real significance is demonstrating 96% accuracy on MNIST classification, showing that quantum speedups for machine learning workloads may be closer to practical reality than previously thought. The authors' hybrid quantum-classical framework represents exactly the kind of pragmatic approach needed to bridge the gap between quantum computing theory and the massive computational demands of modern AI.

Matrix multiplication (MatMul) is the computational backbone of modern machine learning, yet its classical complexity remains a bottleneck for large-scale data processing. We propose a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm for matrix multiplication based on an adaptive configuration of Hadamard tests. By leveraging Quantum Random Access Memory (QRAM) for state preparation, we demonstrate that the complexity of computing the inner product of two vectors can be reduced to $O(\log N)$. We introduce an "Adaptive Stacking" framework that allows the algorithm to dynamically reconfigure its execution pattern from sequential horizontal stacking to massive vertical parallelism based on available qubit resources. This flexibility enables a tunable time-complexity range, theoretically reaching $O(N^2)$ on fault-tolerant systems while maintaining compatibility with near-term hardware. We validate the numerical stability of our approach through a Quantum Machine Learning (QML) simulation, achieving 96% accuracy on the MNIST handwritten digit dataset. Our results suggest that adaptive quantum MatMul provides a viable path toward super-classical efficiency in high-dimensional linear algebra operations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum entanglement between electrons and ions captured at attosecond timescale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a remarkable technical achievement in quantum measurement, capturing the fleeting moments when electrons and ions first become entangled at the attosecond timescale - that's quintillionths of a second. Understanding entanglement formation at such ultrafast timescales could unlock new pathways for creating more stable and controllable quantum systems, which is crucial for advancing quantum computing and quantum sensing technologies. The ability to observe and potentially manipulate these fundamental quantum processes as they happen opens exciting possibilities for engineering better qubits and quantum devices. While this research is still in the fundamental physics realm, it provides critical insights into how nature creates the quantum correlations that quantum technologies depend on.

Quantum mechanics is extremely successful at describing the behavior of matter at the atomic level. This success forces one to accept that certain aspects of physical reality go far beyond our intuition. Among these, none is more intriguing than the concept of quantum entanglement, which mathematically describes how two particles that have at some point in the past interacted with each other retain a memory of this interaction to such an extent that acting on one of the two particles has a measurable influence on the properties of the other particle, even if the two have long ago stopped interacting and may be separated by such a vast distance that communication between them is no longer possible.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Absolute Schmidt number: characterization, detection and resource-theoretic quantification
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a fascinating new perspective on entanglement by identifying quantum states whose Schmidt number - a measure of entanglement dimensionality - cannot be enhanced through any global unitary operation. The concept of "absolute Schmidt number" provides a sharp dividing line between states that represent maximal entanglement resources and those that could potentially be improved, offering new theoretical tools for characterizing and detecting these enhancement opportunities. The authors' development of both witness-based detection methods and resource-theoretic quantification measures could prove valuable for optimizing entanglement utilization in quantum protocols, while their extension to quantum channels opens up intriguing possibilities for understanding how entanglement resources behave under different channel operations. This work adds important nuance to our understanding of entanglement as a quantum resource, suggesting that some entangled states may have untapped potential waiting to be unlocked through clever unitary transformations.

The dimensionality of entanglement, quantified by the Schmidt number, is a valuable resource for a wide range of quantum information processing tasks. In this work, we introduce the notion of the absolute Schmidt number, referring to states whose Schmidt number cannot be increased by any global unitary transformation. We provide a characterization of the set of arbitrary-dimensional states whose Schmidt number is invariant under all global unitaries. Our approach enables us to develop both witness-based and moment-based techniques to detect nonabsolute Schmidt number states which could provide significant operational advantages through Schmidt number enhancement by global unitaries. We next formulate two resource-theoretic measures of nonabsolute Schmidt number states, based respectively on Schmidt number witness and robustness, and demonstrate an operational utility of the latter in a channel discrimination task. Finally, we extend our analysis to quantum channels by introducing a new class of channels that possess the absolute Schmidt number property. We derive a necessary and sufficient condition for identifying when a channel has the absolute Schmidt number property, confining our analysis to the class of covariant channels.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Space-Efficient Quantum Algorithm for Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithms with Resource Estimation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant breakthrough in making quantum attacks on elliptic curve cryptography more feasible by dramatically reducing the number of logical qubits required. The researchers achieved a 37% reduction in qubit requirements for 256-bit curves (from 2124 to 1333 logical qubits), which brings quantum cryptanalysis closer to the realm of practical quantum computers. This advance is particularly important because elliptic curve cryptography secures everything from HTTPS connections to cryptocurrency transactions, and reducing the hardware requirements for breaking these systems affects how quickly organizations need to migrate to post-quantum cryptography. The work demonstrates that algorithmic improvements can be just as crucial as hardware advances in determining when quantum computers will pose a real threat to current cryptographic infrastructure.

Solving the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) is critical for evaluating the quantum security of widely deployed elliptic-curve cryptosystems. Consequently, minimizing the number of logical qubits required to execute this algorithm is a key object. In implementations of Shor's algorithm, the space complexity is largely dictated by the modular inversion operation during point addition. Starting from the extended Euclidean algorithm (EEA), we refine the register-sharing method of Proos and Zalka and propose a space-efficient reversible modular inversion algorithm. We use length registers together with location-controlled arithmetic to store the intermediate variables in a compact form throughout the computation. We then optimize the stepwise update rules and give concrete circuit constructions for the resulting controlled arithmetic components. This leads to a modular inversion circuit that uses $3n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ logical qubits and $204n^2\log_2 n + O(n^2)$ Toffoli gates. By inserting this modular inversion component into the controlled affine point-addition circuit, we obtain a space-efficient algorithm for the ECDLP with $5n + 4\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + O(1)$ qubits and $O(n^3)$ Toffoli gates. In particular, for a 256-bit prime-field curve, our estimate reduces the logical-qubit count to 1333, compared with 2124 in the previous low-width implementation of Häner et al.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Twinning! Quantum ‘Digital Twins’ Tackle Error Correction Task to Speed Path to Reliable Quantum Computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a clever approach to one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges by creating classical "digital twins" that can simulate how errors propagate through large quantum systems. Rather than needing to build and test every error correction scheme on actual quantum hardware—which is expensive and time-consuming—researchers can now rapidly prototype and optimize these critical systems using classical computers. This could significantly accelerate the development of fault-tolerant quantum computers by allowing teams to identify the most promising error correction strategies before implementing them on real quantum processors. The timing is particularly noteworthy as it comes during a week of multiple error correction breakthroughs, suggesting the field is reaching a crucial inflection point in making quantum computers truly reliable.

Insider Brief In a week that has seen a parade of error-correction advances, a team of quantum computing researchers say they have shown a practical way to simulate large, error-prone quantum systems on classical computers, an advance that the scientists suggest could speed the path toward reliable quantum machines. A collaboration involving Amazon Web Services, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Explicit constructions of mutually unbiased bases via Hadamard matrices
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive study tackles one of quantum information's most fundamental yet frustrating puzzles: constructing complete sets of mutually unbiased bases, particularly in dimension 6 where no complete set has ever been found despite decades of searching. The work provides explicit analytical methods for generating MUBs using Hadamard matrices and reveals the deep algebraic structures that make some dimensions tractable while others remain mysteriously resistant. Beyond the theoretical elegance, MUBs are crucial for quantum cryptography protocols, quantum state tomography, and optimal quantum measurements, making these explicit constructions valuable for practical quantum information applications. The authors' systematic computational framework could prove instrumental in either finally cracking the dimension-6 case or understanding why it might be fundamentally impossible.

We present a detailed computational and algebraic study of Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) in finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, with a particular focus on dimensions 2, 3, 4, and the challenging case of 6. Starting from the Hadamard-phase parametrization, we derive explicit analytical conditions for mutual unbiasedness in dimension 4, providing a tractable system of trigonometric constraints on the phase parameters. We then explore a tensor-product construction via Pauli operators, highlighting the algebraic and group-theoretical origin of MUBs in two-qubit systems, and demonstrating how these constructions yield a complete set of 5 MUBs in dimension 4. Extending our approach, we investigate the Fourier-family method in dimension 6, where the absence of a prime-power structure imposes strong rigidity constraints and limits the known constructions to sets of 3 MUBs. We provide a systematic computational framework for testing candidate phase vectors, bridging the gap between analytical insight and numerical exploration. Finally, we generalize the discussion to arbitrary prime-power dimensions, emphasizing the role of finite-field structures, Heisenberg-Weyl operators, and discrete symmetries in generating complete sets of MUBs. Our work offers a transparent, line-by-line verification methodology, highlighting both the geometric and algebraic richness of MUBs, and clarifying why certain dimensions resist full analytical constructions. This study serves as a comprehensive resource for researchers seeking both theoretical understanding and practical construction of MUBs in quantum information science.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Phase Quantum Walk: A Unified Framework for Graph State Distribution in Quantum Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a clever new approach to one of quantum networking's most fundamental challenges: how to efficiently distribute complex entangled states across multiple quantum processors or network nodes. The phase quantum walk method is particularly elegant because it can generate any graph state topology using just simple two-qubit operations, moving beyond the limitation of previous methods that could only create basic GHZ states. The experimental validation on IBM's hardware is especially significant, as it provides the first real-world confirmation that distribution fidelity remains consistent regardless of the target graph's complexity - a counterintuitive result that could dramatically simplify quantum network protocol design. This framework could prove essential for scaling up modular quantum computers and enabling sophisticated distributed quantum algorithms that require precise control over multi-party entanglement patterns.

Distributing arbitrary graph states across quantum networks is a central challenge for modular quantum computing and measurement-based quantum communication. I introduce the phase quantum walk (PQW), a discrete-time quantum walk in which the conventional position-permuting shift operator is replaced by a diagonal conditional phase (CZ) gate, enabling distribution of arbitrary graph states, not merely GHZ states, from elementary two-qubit resources. The Byproduct Lemma shows that each walk step teleports edge entanglement with a correctable Pauli byproduct; the Coin Invariance Theorem proves that the optimal fidelity F*(C,E) = F*(H,E) for all unitary coins C and noise channels E, with closed-form expressions F_dep = (1 - 3p/4)^k and F_pd = ((1 + sqrt(1 - p))/2)^k. Analytical correction formulas are derived for tree graphs (general theorem) and ring graphs (C4 case study), with F = 1.0 verified across eight topologies (up to 4096 outcomes). Hardware validation on ibm marrakesh (IBM Heron r2, CZ-native) yields F_cl = 0.924 for |GHZ4> and 0.922 for |L4>, statistically identical, providing the first experimental confirmation that fidelity is independent of graph topology as predicted by the Coin Invariance Theorem.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Constrained Quantum Optimization via Iterative Warm-Start XY-Mixers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of QAOA's most persistent headaches: how to efficiently handle hard constraints while still getting good performance from the algorithm. The authors cleverly combine two powerful techniques - XY-mixers that keep solutions valid and warm-starting that guides the search toward promising regions - by developing new mixer Hamiltonians that maintain crucial mathematical properties needed for convergence. Their Iterative Warm-Starting approach shows dramatic improvements on classic optimization problems like Max-k-Cut and TSP, boosting the probability of finding optimal solutions by orders of magnitude compared to standard methods. This represents a significant step toward making QAOA more practical for real-world constrained optimization problems, where maintaining feasibility throughout the quantum evolution is often as important as finding good solutions.

The Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is a leading hybrid heuristic for combinatorial optimization, but efficiently handling hard constraints remains a significant challenge. XY-mixers successfully confine quantum state evolution to a feasible subspace, such as the Hamming-weight-1 sector for one-hot constraints. On the contrary, warm-starting biases the search toward promising regions based on preliminary solutions. Combining these two techniques requires maintaining the essential alignment between the initial state and the mixer Hamiltonian to preserve convergence guarantees. Previous work demonstrated warm-starting with XY-mixers via a biased initial state, but relying only on standard mixer Hamiltonians. Consequently, the initial state is no longer a ground state of the mixer. In this work, we overcome these limitations by formulating a warm-started XY-mixer Hamiltonian for one-hot constraints and proving its ground-state properties. Furthermore, we provide a shallow circuit implementation suitable for NISQ implementations. We embed the warm-starting into a classical heuristic that iteratively updates the bias based on previous samples, called Iterative Warm-Starting (IWS). Extensive numerical simulations on Max-$k$-Cut and Traveling Salesperson Problem instances demonstrate that IWS-QAOA significantly accelerates the solution-finding process, increasing the probability of sampling optimal solutions by orders of magnitude compared to standard XY-QAOA. Finally, we validate our approach on the ibm_boston QPU using hardware-tailored 144-qubit problem instances. By coupling IWS-QAOA with a greedy steepest-descent post-processing strategy to repair infeasible measurements caused by hardware noise, we successfully identify optimal solutions on actual quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Photonic qubit encoding interconversion for heterogeneous quantum networking
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most practical challenges facing quantum networks today: how to connect different types of quantum systems that speak different "languages" for encoding information in photons. The team demonstrates a clever solution that converts between polarization-based and time-bin-based qubit encoding, allowing quantum information to survive transmission through real-world fiber optic cables that would normally scramble polarization states. By successfully preserving quantum entanglement while switching between encoding schemes, this work provides a crucial bridge technology for building heterogeneous quantum networks where IBM's superconducting qubits might need to talk to Google's photonic systems or university research nodes. This kind of quantum "translation" capability is essential for scaling beyond isolated quantum devices toward the interconnected quantum internet that researchers envision.

Quantum information processing, communication, and sensing networks are being developed with various qubit platforms that use different encoding schemes. Connecting quantum network nodes to distribute entanglement requires matching photon qubit basis encoding. In this work, we implement an interconversion protocol which converts photon qubit encoding from the polarization basis to the time-bin basis, transmits the photons through a transport fiber with large fluctuations in polarization, and converts back to polarization encoding for ease of measurement. This interconversion scheme faithfully transmits a polarization Bell state across the transport fiber by converting sources of infidelity to changes in transmission rate. These results illustrate a practical approach for interfacing distinct qubit platforms to enable modular and flexible operation in heterogeneous quantum networks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Emergence of volume-law scaling for entanglement negativity from the Hawking radiation of analogue black holes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research provides the first concrete demonstration that Hawking radiation creates a fundamentally different type of quantum entanglement than previously observed, with implications that extend far beyond theoretical physics into practical quantum simulation. The team shows that entanglement negativity - a key measure of quantum correlations - develops a volume-law scaling rather than the typical logarithmic behavior, suggesting that Hawking radiation creates long-range entangled pairs throughout the black hole's interior and exterior regions. What makes this particularly exciting is that these predictions could be tested in current cold-atom experiments, offering a pathway to probe black hole physics in the laboratory and potentially resolve fundamental questions about information loss during black hole evaporation. This work bridges quantum information theory, black hole physics, and experimental quantum simulation in a way that could illuminate one of the most profound puzzles in modern physics.

The quantum information content of Hawking radiation holds the key to understanding black-hole evaporation and the fate of unitarity. Motivated by recent advances in cold-atom experiments, we develop a lattice-regularization approach aimed at simulating the coarse-grained entanglement scaling of a quantum field in a 1+1D analogue black-hole background. We provide the first concrete demonstration that logarithmic negativity -- an entanglement monotone that typically exhibits a UV-divergent log-scaling for the conformal vacuum -- acquires a UV-finite volume term from the nonlocal correlations seeded by Hawking radiation. We show that this volume term encodes both the number density and spatial distribution of entangled Hawking pairs along the black-hole interior and exterior. We highlight its prospective detection in currently realizable experiments as well as its implications beyond the analogue paradigm, in particular for black-hole thermodynamics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Decoding Realistic Quantum Error Syndrome with Quantum Elements Digital Twins
item.description:
Curator's Take: This AWS article tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing engineering challenges: determining the precise hardware quality and code size needed to achieve practical fault tolerance. By using digital twins to model realistic error syndromes, the research moves beyond theoretical thresholds to provide concrete engineering targets for building useful quantum computers. This work is particularly significant because it bridges the gap between academic quantum error correction theory and the real-world constraints faced by hardware engineers trying to build systems that can actually outperform classical computers. The digital twin approach could accelerate the timeline for fault-tolerant quantum computing by helping companies optimize their hardware development priorities and resource allocation.

Fault-tolerant quantum computing requires quantum error correction (QEC): Encoding one logical qubit into many physical qubits so that, below a threshold error rate, the logical error rate falls rapidly as the code grows. The practical engineering question is: How large must the code be and how good must the hardware be to reach a useful […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Dancing to Invisible Choreography, Quantum Computers Can Balance The Noise
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: noise-induced errors that plague current quantum systems and prevent them from scaling to practical applications. While the "dancing to invisible choreography" metaphor is poetic, the underlying issue is deeply technical - quantum states are extraordinarily fragile and easily disrupted by environmental interference, leading to computational errors that accumulate rapidly as system size grows. The piece appears to focus on error mitigation strategies that could help balance or compensate for this noise, which represents a crucial stepping stone toward fault-tolerant quantum computers. Progress in noise management is essential because even small improvements in error rates can dramatically extend the useful computational time of quantum processors before decoherence destroys the quantum advantage.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Large-scale quantum computers are waiting in the wings. One of the main reasons we don’t have them yet is because quantum hardware is so noisy. This isn’t the type of noise you’d want to shush in a crowded theater. When it comes to computers, noise means errors that crop up […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Infleqtion Delivers Quantum Precision Timing for Mission-Critical Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a significant milestone in bringing quantum sensing technology out of the laboratory and into real-world applications, as Infleqtion's Tiqker quantum optical clock represents one of the first commercial deployments of quantum-enhanced timing systems for critical infrastructure. The partnership with defense contractor Safran Electronics & Defense underscores how quantum timing solutions are moving beyond research demonstrations to address genuine market needs in navigation, communications, and secure operations where GPS denial or jamming poses serious risks. What makes this particularly noteworthy is that precision timing serves as a foundational technology across numerous sectors - from financial trading networks to power grid synchronization - suggesting this could be an early indicator of quantum sensing's broader commercial viability. The focus on mission-critical systems also highlights how quantum technologies are finding their initial footholds in high-value applications where the performance advantages justify the current costs and complexity.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Infleqtion (NYSE: INFQ), a global leader in quantum sensing and quantum computing powered by neutral-atom technology, announced availability of the first quantum-enabled precision timing solution delivered as part of the company’s partnership with global civil and military equipment manufacturer Safran Electronics & Defense. The new solution includes Infleqtion’s Tiqker™ quantum optical […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Demonstrates Below-Threshold Error Mitigation in Photonic Quantum Computing For First Time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a critical milestone for photonic quantum computing, as QuiX Quantum becomes the first to demonstrate error mitigation below the fault tolerance threshold using photons as qubits. While superconducting and trapped-ion systems have dominated recent quantum computing headlines, this achievement positions photonic approaches as serious contenders for scalable quantum systems, leveraging photons' natural resistance to decoherence and room-temperature operation. The "below threshold" designation is particularly significant because it indicates error rates low enough that quantum error correction could theoretically work, bringing photonic quantum computers closer to the realm of practical fault-tolerant computation. This success could reshape the quantum hardware landscape by validating an alternative approach that offers unique advantages for certain quantum applications, especially those requiring network connectivity.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum, a leading provider of photonic quantum computing hardware, today announced it has demonstrated “below threshold” error mitigation for the first time on a photonic quantum computer, suppressing physical qubit errors to the level compatible with scalable, fault‑tolerant quantum computing. The achievement marks the first time a European company […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Novel approach to quantum error correction portends a scalable future for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough from the University of Sydney tackles one of quantum computing's most daunting challenges: the massive overhead required for error correction, which currently demands hundreds or thousands of physical qubits to create a single reliable logical qubit. Dr. Williamson's novel "gauging logical operators" approach promises to dramatically reduce this overhead, potentially making fault-tolerant quantum computers practical with far fewer physical qubits than previously thought necessary. Published in Nature Physics, this work represents a crucial step toward scaling quantum computers beyond the current noisy intermediate-scale quantum era into truly useful, error-corrected systems. The reduced qubit requirements could accelerate the timeline for quantum advantage in real-world applications by making large-scale quantum computers more feasible with near-term hardware capabilities.

A University of Sydney quantum physicist has developed a new approach to quantum error correction that could significantly reduce the number of physical qubits required to build large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers. The study, co-authored by Dr. Dominic Williamson from the School of Physics, is titled "Low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computation by gauging logical operators" and published in Nature Physics.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development represents a crucial step toward making quantum computing more practical and accessible by shrinking trapped ion systems from laboratory-scale setups to portable devices. Trapped ions are among the most promising platforms for quantum computing due to their exceptional coherence times and high-fidelity quantum operations, but until now they've required extensive laboratory infrastructure with vacuum systems, lasers, and electromagnetic controls. The miniaturization breakthrough could accelerate the deployment of quantum technologies beyond research labs, potentially enabling field-deployable quantum sensors, portable atomic clocks for navigation systems, and eventually bringing quantum computing closer to real-world applications. This trend toward compact quantum hardware mirrors the evolution of classical electronics and suggests we're moving from the "room-sized computer" era of quantum technology toward more practical implementations.

Portable device could find applications in quantum computing and optical clocks The post Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Useful quantum computers could be built with as few as 10,000 qubits, team finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a dramatic shift in quantum computing timelines, potentially accelerating useful quantum computers from decades away to within this decade. The 100x reduction in qubit requirements — from millions down to 10,000-20,000 qubits — stems from breakthrough error reduction techniques that could make fault-tolerant quantum computing far more achievable with current and near-term hardware capabilities. Given that IBM and Google are already approaching the 1,000+ qubit range, this finding suggests we might see practically useful quantum computers tackling real-world problems much sooner than the quantum community has traditionally expected. The collaboration between Caltech theorists and the startup Oratomic also highlights how academic-industry partnerships are accelerating the path from quantum research to commercial reality.

Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today's rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require millions of qubits to work properly (qubits being the quantum equivalent to 1's and 0's in classical computers), the new results indicate that a fully realized quantum computer could be built with as few as 10,000 to 20,000 qubits. The need for fewer qubits means that quantum computers could, in theory, be operational by the end of the decade.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks a crucial milestone in making quantum computing practical and scalable, as silicon-based systems can leverage the massive existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure that powers our modern electronics. The achievement of logical operations - where multiple physical qubits work together to create more reliable "logical qubits" - addresses one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: error correction. Silicon's naturally long coherence times, combined with its industrial compatibility, position it as a serious contender against other qubit technologies like superconducting circuits and trapped ions. This could accelerate the path toward fault-tolerant quantum computers by building on decades of silicon chip manufacturing expertise rather than requiring entirely new production methods.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computer accurately simulates real magnetic materials, reproducing national laboratory data
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a crucial milestone in quantum computing's journey from laboratory curiosity to practical scientific tool, demonstrating that current quantum computers can now accurately reproduce real-world experimental data from national laboratories. The ability to simulate magnetic materials with quantum computers addresses one of the most computationally challenging problems in materials science, where classical computers often fail to capture the complex quantum interactions that govern magnetic behavior. This breakthrough suggests we're entering an era where quantum computers can begin complementing and eventually surpassing traditional computational methods for materials discovery, potentially accelerating the development of everything from more efficient permanent magnets to novel quantum materials for future technologies. The validation against established experimental data gives researchers confidence that quantum simulations can now serve as reliable tools for exploring materials that would be impossible or prohibitively expensive to synthesize and test physically.

Studying and designing novel materials is a central application of quantum mechanics. Chemists, materials scientists, and physicists focus on subtle interactions in quantum materials and to uncover them they rely on sophisticated computational and experimental techniques. Computer simulations that connect microscopic quantum interactions to measurable material properties complement experimental data to connect structure to function—but classical computers can struggle to simulate those properties. Fortunately, scientists today have a new tool in their toolbox: quantum computers.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Unlocking scalable entanglement will enable next-generation quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: creating and maintaining entanglement at the scale needed for practical quantum advantage. While the summary touches on quantum computing's transformative potential, the real breakthrough will come when researchers can reliably generate entangled states across hundreds or thousands of qubits simultaneously. Current quantum systems struggle with maintaining coherence as they scale up, making this entanglement scalability problem the key bottleneck preventing quantum computers from moving beyond today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices to truly fault-tolerant systems. Solving this challenge would unlock the path to those revolutionary applications in drug discovery, materials science, and cryptography that have captured the world's imagination.

Quantum computing promises to transform our world in rapid, radical and revolutionary ways: solving in seconds problems that would take classical computers years, accelerating the discovery of new medicines, creating sustainable materials, optimizing complex systems, and strengthening cybersecurity. It does so using qubits, the quantum counterparts of classical bits, which can occupy multiple states simultaneously and enable a fundamentally new kind of computation.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws. Unlike typical quantum systems where entanglement grows with the boundary area between regions, Motzkin chains exhibit logarithmic scaling that makes them notoriously difficult to simulate on classical computers, yet they hold deep connections to topological phases and even string theory through AdS/CFT correspondence. The researchers' proposed Rydberg atom platform offers a realistic experimental route to create these mathematical constructs in the lab, potentially opening doors to studying a whole new class of highly entangled quantum phases that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work exemplifies how programmable quantum simulators are becoming powerful tools for exploring exotic quantum matter that exists at the edge of what's computationally tractable.

Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: NRFC Investment Accelerates SQC’s Atomic-Scale Manufacturing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Silicon Quantum Computing's unique atomic-scale manufacturing approach, which positions them as the sole company worldwide capable of fabricating quantum chips with atomic precision. The AUD$20 million from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation not only validates SQC's silicon-based quantum technology but also strengthens Australia's position in the global quantum race, particularly in the critical hardware manufacturing sector where precision at the atomic level remains one of the field's greatest technical challenges. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's claim of already delivering quantum-enhanced AI products to enterprise and government clients today, suggesting their technology has moved beyond pure research into practical applications. This combination of manufacturing precision and commercial readiness could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computing deployment, especially in applications where silicon-based quantum systems offer advantages over other qubit technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) has invested AUD$20 million in Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC), a global leader in the field of quantum computing. SQC is the only company in the world capable of manufacturing quantum chips with atomic precision while delivering quantum enhanced-AI products for enterprises and governments today. It is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Global control via quantum actuators
item.description:
